sentences.text: Well, I remember that my father did go to war when Poland went to war with Germany.
And he didn't come right away back.
I remember when we were bombed, when the shells were dropping on us.
And I remember being in bomb shelters and saying the Shema Yisrael.
I didn't know what it meant, but my aunt, my mother, everybody says, children pray, whoever knows how to pray.
And I remember sitting in this bomb shelters and praying, Shema Yisrael.
And we were-- I don't how long we didn't see our father.
The word came that yes, he was alive.
And one day, he appeared in his civilian clothes.
And we were told, the children, not to tell anyone, not even the neighbors, that he is back.
I guess he was a Polish officer.
And if the word would get out to the Germans that he-- Polish officer is back at that time-- that was before the Jewish situation with the armbands and-- and stopping us from going to schools.
But knowing that he was an officer, they might take him away.
So for a while, he couldn't go out, and he didn't go anywhere.
I remember our uncles and aunts were coming to us, and some other people, to visit.
Little by little, I guess, he went out.
And then, before we went to ghetto, I remember the pious Hasidic Jews being rushed down through the streets, one side of their faces and the heads being shaved, and with their hands up.
They were being run through the streets, followed by the Germans.
And the Poles used to stand on the sides, applaud, and laugh.
I remember when the Germans used to announce that all the silver and gold and furs-- the Jewish people have to prepare them on the tables that they will come in the evening and gather them.
I remember gorgeous furs being thrown from balconies, and we used to tramp on them so the German would not get them in a-- in a perfect condition.
I remember when they came to our house.
And they took our silver candelabras, and they took off my mother's rings from her fingers.
And I remember my father being hit by a German.
I don't know what he said or what he'd done.
I know we were all scared.
And we lived in a building that were-- all Jewish people lived there.
We lived in a neighborhood that was predominantly Jewish.
We lived on the [POLISH] which is next to the Wawel, near the Bernardine church.
But we also lived-- that was close to Dietlawska street, which was very orthodox neighborhood.
And I remember just screaming and-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] With the time when we went to ghettos, I remember you couldn't take much.
And you see them, everybody with their packages, whatever they could carry.
Some of them were furniture pieces on wagons, to go into the ghetto.
As a child, I knew it wasn't normal, but I couldn't understand.
I thought it would be fun to be together with some new friends, new children.
My mother said, you be able to find new friends, and you be able to maybe study.
And I remember also studying ballet with a famous ballerina and a movie star.
You know, I'm trying to remember her name.
I saw her after the war.
She lived through the war.
But she taught me ballet.
She had classes in ghetto.
A Jewish woman.
She certainly did not look Jewish, and she-- she was a young person at that time.
She must have been in her very early 20s.
In ghetto, in-- you know, it-- it was-- there was a public place where you could sort of perform and gather a lot of people, and she took part of it.
And we used to have certain time of the day.
She used to give us lessons.
I wasn't the only one.
There were a few of us.
She had, like, a little Victrola with a record.
Yes.
You know, it felt good.
It felt that pretty soon I'd be able to perform in the-- in the free world.
I was learning.
I was looking toward future.
I was-- for some reason, I always knew deep in my heart that I will live, that I will come through.
It was hard to when I was separated from the family, from my parents and my sisters and my brother.
But I was always shelter, whatever camp I was, by the adults around me.
And they even try to give me their extra ration so I would be better nourished, because, you know, our rations were very meager.
I mean, they weren't even fit for-- we don't treat like-- animals like this.
You know, there-- there were songs that were composed in the ghettos.
I don't.
I don't remember them now.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No, I don't remember them.
Yes.
Yes.
We had, like, a little group.
They were Polish songs.
I don't remember exactly what songs they were.
But I know toward the end, we always sang [NON-ENGLISH].
And we knew the words to it in Polish, and my sisters.
Not really, no.
I think it was the beginning of '42 when I went to Plaszow ghetto.
I remember marching.
We had to march all the way from Krakow to Plaszow.
I have no idea how far this was.
It seemed quite far to me.
Going to the Plaszow ghetto, I remember I was turning away my head because they were shooting-- again, very religious Jews.
They were in-- by the deep, deep grave with their hands up, the yarmulkes on their heads.
They used to shoot them.
I didn't want to see it.
I didn't want to face this thing.
So I used to turn my head away if I could because during their hanging of the Jewish people, or shooting, they wanted you to face it.
They wanted you to see it, to watch it, observe it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they used to punish you with death when you were very hungry and you stole a potato.
They had bunkers of potatoes, I remember, in Plaszow.
But they used to bury the potatoes for winter like they did the coal, preparing them so they wouldn't froze.
They were guarded by the Germans, but a lot of people were hungry.
And some of them, once in a while, used to sneak in, steal some potatoes, share them with others, if they were not caught.
But if they were caught, they were shot.
Oh, yes, I saw them hanging.
I didn't see them being shot by the bunker where the potatoes were.
But some other people did, and I remember hearing it in the barracks.
And you know, then the one day, there was a rumor that they were liquidating this Biezanow camp, where my parents were, that they were bringing everyone into Plaszow.
And in Plaszow, they had a couple barracks for children.
They create, like, a nursery.
They had couple adults watching them and feed them.
Yes, but I was not in this barrack.
I was in the barrack with the adults.
They had barracks according to the company that you work.
You gathered with the same people.
And the children were separate.
When they brought the children from the camp Biezanow, and the adults, they separated.
The children went to this nursery in Plaszow, and the adults were shipped out to a different camp.
I remember getting to the gates, and this is where-- when I saw my parents for the last time until the liberation.
And my mother walk up to an SS man.
He was the commander of the Plaszow ghetto.
Yes.
He was a very big.
His name was Goth.
And my mother and father commanded a very good German.
And she said, I want my children.
Leave me here with my children, or give me my children.
And he told her, you better move away from me-- in German-- or you will be shot.
My mother would not let him just-- show him that she was afraid.
But my father and other people separate her away from him, took her back.
They were shipped with a transport of all these people that came from this Biezanow camp the following morning.
But my sisters were left where I was, in the Plaszow camp.
And I used to go and see them.
Early in the morning, before even the appell-- you had to stand up and be counted every morning-- to share with them my ration.
And I used to go and see them in the afternoon, when I used to come late afternoon from work.
They were couple women to each barrack.
Yes, definitely.
And the children were being disciplined to make their own beds, and to provide for their own little needs.
And they were fed meagerly, but yes, they did get some ration.
And after being there-- I don't remember if it was a month, six weeks, or two months.
But when-- one day, they took us all from this camp to one tremendous appellplatz.
And we were surrounded with SS men and machine guns.
And they put a tremendously loud music through the loud speakers.
Classical music.
They came by trucks near the barracks where the children were, and they took the children out.
There were many people that had children in those barracks because they had to give the children being separately.
It was like a nursery.
They sat.
While you go to work, the children would be in the nursery.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- the-- the screaming and the crying, you can't imagine.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We heard after a while that they took the children to Auschwitz and that they cremated them.
There must have been about three or four trucks of children.
I don't know how many children were in those barracks.
The total amount, I don't.
I know that this is how I lost my three sisters.
They were taken out of the barracks, and they were taken out to the crematoriums, to Auschwitz.
And I know that after the war, my father walked from Krakow to Auschwitz because he was liberated by the Russians.
And therefore, he was liberated in 1944.
He walked because he heard that there were some people that survived in Auschwitz, some children.
Perhaps, perhaps, the children were spared and saved.
But they weren't.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They were cremated.
And then when they liquidated Plaszow, the ghetto, I was-- I guess they were liquidating many camps.
When they were liquidating Plaszow, I went to Auschwitz.
We were again standing.
There's always standing and counting us.
Waiting and standing outside, no matter what weather it was.
I saw my brother.
He threw me bread that-- we used to gather bread to go on our road.
Who knows what the road was for us.
And so many people threw themselves on this loaf of bread that I never got the bread.
And that was the last time I saw him until after the liberation.
Yes, to some extent.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We saw-- we heard that it was a concentration camp and that it had crematorium.
And when I came out from the train and they unloaded us again, there were dogs and chaos and officers in their boots and yelling and shouting and pushing us.
I remember this is when my long braids were gone.
They did not shave my head, but they cut it to a boy's style.
I guess it's in style today, but at that time, it was not in style.
But my head was not shaved.
I went in one room, with others, naturally, and I was totally undressed to be naked.
And I went through a segregation, to the left and the right.
They were bunch of officers.
I don't even remember their names or their faces.
I was afraid to look at them.
But one of the officers, as I was going through, totally nude, call me with his finger.
And he ask me in German, [GERMAN], which means, what is your name?
And I said, [NON-ENGLISH] is Immergluck, which means 'always lucky.' And he says to me, [GERMAN], which means, 'Are you always lucky?' He asked me, and I said, [NON-ENGLISH].
And he send me to the side where was not going, obviously, to the crematorium.
And I don't remember if it was left or right.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Then we went into the showers.
And right away, the water was coming, and we were going through some kind of a disinfections in the showers.
And I was issued a stripe uniform and a pair of wooden shoes.
No underwear, nothing.
At that time.
12 or maybe 13.
You, know, at that time, I only thought of survival.
So I guess it's like a-- you think what to do, how to act, to survive.
And the first thing, you were very obedient to their commands or demands because if you rebel, goodbye.
There were no debates or ifs or buts.
They were in command of your life.
Well, I wasn't the only one naked.
Everybody in the line before me and after me was nude.
And this is also where I've seen mothers with children being separated.
And again the chaos was terrible, the cries and the lamenting.
I was happy that I was going to the line, I was told, where we'll receive the number, which I have now on my left arm, the tattoo number.
That I would be working, that I will be alive for little bit longer.
Who knows?
We were hoping that the war will finish.
We were hoping that some kind of a help will come and liberate us.
I went to Auschwitz.
I had one aunt with me.
Her name was Erna.
Immergluck.
She was my aunt through marriage, in a sense that she married my father's brother.
She came with me from Plaszow, so I felt kind of secured somewhat.
She also went through this transport and-- I mean, through the selections.
And she also went to the side to receive the number.
But you know, I don't remember her receiving a number.
I just don't remember many things.
I try to block out so much.
Well, we were dress.
We received the-- the stripe skirt and the long jacket and the wooden shoes.
And I remember we stayed for hours again in this appellplatz.
And it was very cold.
It was winter.
Then we were march on a front of some kind of a building.
And again, we were standing in a line to receive this number, this tattoo.
And I didn't know what a tattoo was.
I was told by the people that were guiding us to go in and stop.
And these were the people that were before, came earlier to Auschwitz, and they were the overseers.
But they were also prisoners.
I was only told that once I received a number, that means I will be left to live, and I will be working.
But when I received the-- a number, my arm was swollen.
And I was rubbing it.
I was hoping to rub this out.
But it never did.
I didn't understand how deep this went or-- that this is a tattoo that will stay with me.
Yeah.
Can you see it now?
And I've seen man and then receive the tattoo number on their forehand.
On their forehand.
And I was told that I bared somebody else's number already that did not have the A series, that there were 26,284 people before me that were tattoo, but without the A number.
I don't know how factual this is.
I have no idea.
I tried.
When I came first United States in 1949, I made some wonderful friends with young people that used to go to Michigan State University.
And at that time, they didn't know much about survivors in concentration camps and the numbers.
And I was invited to formal dances.
I was young and so happy to be alive.
When I was invited, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was trying to cover this up with makeup at first I was ashamed of it.
The young people didn't understand.
